Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings after my extracurriculars, usually reading through notes and course materials since that's how I learn best. With 17 online courses to juggle, I couldn't really give each one the attention it probably deserved. I'd spend maybe a couple hours here and there when I could, but it wasn't super structured. Most weeks I managed around 21 hours total, but it felt like I was just going through the motions a lot of the time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. If there was a deadline coming up, I'd suddenly become way more focused and log in more frequently. Otherwise, I'd let things slide and study whenever I felt like it, which wasn't often enough. Regular times didn't really work for me because my schedule with extracurriculars and limited resources meant I had to be flexible. Some weeks were intense, others I barely touched anything.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or just habit at this point—I'd log in to check if anything new was posted or if I was missing something important. Sometimes I'd get notifications about discussion posts and that would prompt me to go see what was happening. Honestly, a lot of times I logged in without much purpose, just scrolling through to feel like I was doing something productive even when I wasn't really motivated.
Q4: Yeah, after a while logging in just became something I did automatically, like checking social media. I'd open the LMS during breaks or when I was bored, which happened a lot. It became routine because I had so many courses that I felt like I needed to stay on top of things somehow. The discussion posts helped too—seeing peer interactions made me want to at least appear active even if I wasn't fully engaged mentally.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely most useful for me since I'm a read-write learner. I'd download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlighting and taking notes. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too because seeing other students' perspectives sometimes clarified things I was confused about. Videos were okay but I found myself zoning out during them more often than not.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the supplementary videos—I'd start watching them but my mind would wander and I'd just skip ahead or close them entirely. Some of the optional readings I'd save for later and then never actually get to them. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what was absolutely necessary for assignments and exams. The extra stuff just felt like too much when I was already struggling to stay motivated.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be graded on or if it actually helped me understand a concept I was stuck on. If I could see the practical application or knew it would show up on an exam, I'd put in the effort. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade just felt pointless, especially when I was already bored with most of the content.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore without consequences, so I usually did ignore them. Required ones had this weight to them—like I knew I had to do them even if I didn't want to. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it eventually but that rarely happened. The required activities at least gave me structure, which I needed since my motivation was pretty low most of the time.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download the PDFs and reading materials, then study from my phone or printed copies when I didn't have good internet access. I also used my own notes extensively and sometimes looked up concepts on free websites when the course materials didn't make sense. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded materials for hours, and none of that would show in the system. I'd also study with classmates in person, quizzing each other on concepts, which was really helpful but completely invisible. Sometimes I'd spend a whole afternoon working through problems on paper before even opening my laptop. The LMS only saw a fraction of what I actually did.
Q11: Yeah, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a document or resource once, download it immediately, and then work from the downloaded version for days or weeks. It was easier on my data and meant I could study anywhere without needing internet. The LMS would show one access, but I might have read that same document ten times offline. It just made more practical sense given my situation.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging felt impossible. When I was really bored with the content or when multiple deadlines from different courses piled up, I'd just shut down mentally. The CBT sessions I had helped a bit with managing those feelings, but sometimes the material just didn't interest me at all. Low motivation plus boring content equals me staring at my screen without absorbing anything.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from extracurriculars or stressed about money and resources, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd log in but just skim through things without really engaging. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between doing things properly and just getting them done. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it probably was, and confusion about assignments sometimes made me avoid them entirely.
Q14: Oh, all the time. I'd have videos playing in the background while doing other things, or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Sometimes I'd open readings and just stare at the words without processing anything because my brain was elsewhere. The LMS would register my activity, but mentally I was checked out. It's like being physically present but not actually there.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they seemed overwhelming or boring. I knew assignments were important, but I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure and wait until the last minute. Sometimes I avoided discussion posts because I didn't feel like I had anything valuable to contribute. The procrastination was partly about low motivation and partly about not wanting to face things that stressed me out.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd be pretty inactive until a deadline approached, then suddenly I'd be logging in constantly, watching videos at 2x speed, and frantically completing assignments. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could get myself to actually do things. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. They were stressful but necessary for someone with my motivation levels.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I was doing okay and could relax a bit, which sometimes meant I'd engage less afterward. Critical feedback either motivated me to try harder or made me want to avoid that course entirely, depending on my mood. Honestly, I didn't always check feedback right away because I was nervous about what it might say.
Q18: The course structure mattered a lot. When things were clearly laid out with obvious deadlines and requirements, I could manage better. But with 17 courses, some were structured better than others, and the inconsistency was frustrating. Courses that had regular weekly patterns were easier to keep up with than ones that dumped everything at once. Structure helped compensate for my lack of internal motivation.
Q19: Having clear reading materials made engagement easier since that's how I learn best. Discussion forums being active also helped because I could learn from peers without always having to do extra work myself. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when there were too many different types of activities to keep track of. Also, courses that relied heavily on videos were harder for me to stay focused on.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who logs in a lot out of habit or anxiety but struggles to actually complete things. Maybe they're checking what's due, reading discussions, watching some videos, but then procrastinating on the actual assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes being in the LMS is just a way to feel like you're doing something without actually doing the hard work.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline that never showed up in the logs—reading downloaded materials, making notes, studying with friends. My exam score was decent even though my attendance was low because I found ways to learn that worked for me outside the system. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens, especially for people who prefer reading and writing over digital interaction.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns like when someone is active and what they click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is actually understanding the material or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline studying, the mental effort, the conversations with classmates. It's like measuring how long someone sits at a desk without knowing if they're actually working.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious about the material and thinking about it even when you don't have to. For me, true engagement has been rare this semester—I've mostly just been going through the motions to get things done. Real engagement would mean I'm not bored, and honestly, that hasn't happened much.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how bored and unmotivated I've felt throughout most of this course, or how much I've relied on offline studying to get by. It wouldn't capture the mental effort of pushing through when I really didn't want to, or the times I learned something meaningful from a quick conversation with a classmate. It also wouldn't show that despite my low motivation, I still managed to perform okay because I found workarounds that suited my learning style.
